---
layout: default
---

**CI/CD Pipeline for a Static Website**

_DevOps Intern Journey - Technical Walkthrough_

Level: Beginner | Duration: 25 Days | Author: Kazeem Jimoh

# 1\. Overview

Objective: Set up a basic Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipeline using GitHub Actions for automatically deploying a static website to AWS S3 or Netlify.

### Tech Stack

- Git & GitHub
- GitHub Actions (CI/CD)
- AWS S3 or Netlify
- Bash/YAML scripting

### Purpose of this document

This is a step-by-step technical log of how the pipeline was built, so it can be reverted to, reused, or handed off to someone else picking up the project.

# 2\. Progress Tracker

| **Day** | **Task**                     | **Status** | **Notes**                                                                                                      | **Blockers**                |
| ------- | ---------------------------- | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| 1-3     | Git basics, repo setup       | Done       | Configured Git identity, created GitHub repo, practiced add/commit/push, created branch, opened & merged PR    | None                        |
| 4-6     | Build static site (HTML/CSS) | Done       | Created index.html and style.css, verified locally in browser, committed and pushed to repo                    | None                        |
| 7-10    | Configure GitHub Actions     | Done       | Built GitHub Actions workflow deploying to AWS S3 via OIDC role assumption (no long-lived keys)                | Several - resolved, see 5.6 |
| 11-14   | Deploy to AWS S3 or Netlify  | Done       | Verified full push-to-live loop end-to-end, including deletions syncing correctly                              | None                        |
| 15-17   | Rollback/error strategy      | Done       | S3 versioning enabled, git revert path tested, pre-deploy sanity check added and verified to block bad deploys | None                        |
| 18-20   | Custom domain (optional)     | Done       | HTTPS custom domain via ACM + CloudFront + OAC, bucket fully locked down, cross-account DNS delegation         | Several - resolved, see 8.7 |
| 21-22   | Log monitoring               | Done       | CloudFront standard logging to S3, CloudWatch metrics confirmed, alarm + SNS email alert on 5xxErrorRate       | None                        |
| 23      | Final polish                 | Done       | Added README.md, final smoke test passed, repo reviewed                                                        | None                        |
| 24      | Documentation                | Done       | This document + repo README.md                                                                                 | None                        |
| 25      | Final review                 | Done       | Full pipeline reviewed end-to-end, project complete                                                            | None                        |

# 3\. Day 1-3: Git Basics & Repo Setup

## 3.1 Install & Verify Git

\$ git --version  
git version 2.43.0

## 3.2 Configure Git Identity

\$ git config --global user.name "Kazeem"  
\$ git config --global user.email "<holaryinka5050@gmail.com>"

## 3.3 Create the GitHub Repository

- Went to GitHub → New Repository
- Named it: cicd-static-site
- Initialized with a README

## 3.4 Clone Repository Locally

\$ git clone <https://github.com/<username>/cicd-static-site.git>  
\$ cd cicd-static-site

## 3.5 Practice Core Git Loop

\$ git status  
\$ git add .  
\$ git commit -m "Initial commit"  
\$ git push origin main

## 3.6 Branch → PR → Merge Workflow

\$ git checkout -b test-branch  
\# made a small change  
\$ git add .  
\$ git commit -m "Test branch commit"  
\$ git push origin test-branch

Opened a Pull Request on GitHub from test-branch into main, reviewed the diff, and merged it. This mirrors the workflow the CI/CD pipeline will trigger on later (Day 7-10).

## 3.7 Screenshots

## ![](data:image/png;base64,)

_git --version output_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Merged Pull Request_

## 3.8 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Repo initialized, Git workflow fundamentals confirmed, comfortable with branch/PR/merge
- Blockers: None
- Key learning: Branch → PR → merge is the same pattern GitHub Actions will hook into

# 4\. Day 4-6: Build Static Site (HTML/CSS)

## 4.1 Objective

Build a simple static website (HTML + CSS) that will later be deployed automatically through the CI/CD pipeline.

## 4.2 Project Files Created

- index.html - main page markup
- style.css - styling for the page

## 4.3 index.html

&lt;html lang="en"&gt;  
&lt;!doctype html&gt;

&lt;html lang="en"&gt;

&lt;head&gt;

&lt;meta charset="UTF-8" /&gt;

&lt;meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" /&gt;

&lt;title&gt;Cloud Journey&lt;/title&gt;

&lt;link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css" /&gt;

&lt;/head&gt;

&lt;body&gt;

&lt;header&gt;

&lt;p&gt;DevOps · Cloud&lt;/p&gt;

&lt;h1&gt;Learning to ship with CI/CD&lt;/h1&gt;

&lt;p class="lead"&gt;

A small static site, deployed automatically on every push to main.

&lt;/p&gt;

&lt;/header&gt;

&lt;main&gt;

&lt;section&gt;

&lt;h2&gt;Where I'm at&lt;/h2&gt;

&lt;p&gt;

Working through cloud and DevOps fundamentals - version control,

automated pipelines, and hosting on AWS. This repo is part of that

practice.

&lt;/p&gt;

&lt;/section&gt;

&lt;section&gt;

&lt;h2&gt;Stack&lt;/h2&gt;

&lt;ul&gt;

&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Git & GitHub&lt;/strong&gt; - source control&lt;/li&gt;

&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;GitHub Actions&lt;/strong&gt; - CI/CD on push to main&lt;/li&gt;

&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;AWS S3&lt;/strong&gt; - static site hosting&lt;/li&gt;

&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;OIDC&lt;/strong&gt; - auth without long-lived keys&lt;/li&gt;

&lt;/ul&gt;

&lt;/section&gt;

&lt;/main&gt;

&lt;footer&gt;

&lt;p&gt;Deployed via CI/CD - GitHub Actions → AWS S3 → CloudFront&lt;/p&gt;

&lt;/footer&gt;

&lt;/body&gt;

&lt;/html&gt;

## 4.4 style.css

:root {

\--bg: #0d0d0d;

\--surface: #161616;

\--border: #2a2a2a;

\--text: #e8e8e8;

\--muted: #888;

\--accent: #7c6af7;

}

\* {

_margin_: 0;

_padding_: 0;

_box-sizing_: border-box;

}

body {

_font-family_:

system-ui,

\-apple-system,

sans-serif;

_background_: var(--bg);

_color_: var(--text);

_line-height_: 1.6;

_min-height_: 100vh;

_display_: flex;

_flex-direction_: column;

}

header {

_padding_: 4rem 1.5rem 3rem;

_max-width_: 36rem;

_margin_: 0 auto;

_width_: 100%;

}

header p {

_color_: var(--accent);

_font-size_: 0.85rem;

_margin-bottom_: 0.75rem;

}

header h1 {

_font-size_: 1.75rem;

_font-weight_: 600;

_letter-spacing_: -0.02em;

_margin-bottom_: 1rem;

}

header .lead {

_color_: var(--muted);

_font-size_: 1rem;

}

main {

_flex_: 1;

_max-width_: 36rem;

_margin_: 0 auto;

_width_: 100%;

_padding_: 0 1.5rem 3rem;

}

section {

_border-top_: 1px solid var(--border);

_padding_: 2rem 0;

}

section h2 {

_font-size_: 0.75rem;

_font-weight_: 500;

_text-transform_: uppercase;

_letter-spacing_: 0.08em;

_color_: var(--muted);

_margin-bottom_: 1rem;

}

section p {

_color_: var(--muted);

_font-size_: 0.95rem;

}

section p + p {

_margin-top_: 0.75rem;

}

ul {

_list-style_: none;

_display_: flex;

_flex-direction_: column;

_gap_: 0.5rem;

}

ul li {

_font-size_: 0.9rem;

_color_: var(--muted);

_padding-left_: 1rem;

_border-left_: 2px solid var(--border);

}

ul li strong {

_color_: var(--text);

_font-weight_: 500;

}

footer {

_border-top_: 1px solid var(--border);

_padding_: 1.5rem;

_text-align_: center;

_font-size_: 0.8rem;

_color_: var(--muted);

}

## 4.5 Local Verification

Open index.html directly in the browser to confirm the page renders correctly before committing .

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## _Rendered static site in browser_

## 4.6 Commit & Push

\$ git add .  
\$ git commit -m "Add static site (HTML/CSS)"  
\$ git push origin main

## 4.7 Screenshots

## ![](data:image/png;base64,)

_git commit/push terminal output_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## _GitHub repo showing pushed files_

## 4.8 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Static site built, verified locally, pushed to GitHub
- Blockers: None
- Key learning: Keeping the static site simple now makes it easier to isolate CI/CD issues later, since any deploy failure in Day 7-14 will clearly be a pipeline problem, not a site problem

# 5\. Day 7-10: Configure GitHub Actions

## 5.1 Objective

Build a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically deploys the static site to AWS S3 on every push to main. Chose AWS S3 over Netlify to stay fully within the AWS ecosystem and build transferable DevOps skills (IAM, S3, OIDC federation).

## 5.2 Decision: S3 over Netlify

Netlify was the simpler default, but S3 was chosen deliberately: it gives hands-on experience with IAM, bucket policies, and CI-to-cloud authentication, and sets up the later custom domain (Day 18-20) and log monitoring (Day 21-22) tasks to build naturally on CloudFront and S3 access logs.

## 5.3 Infrastructure Setup

### S3 Bucket

- Created bucket with static website hosting enabled
- Index document: index.html
- Bucket policy set for public read access (acceptable for a learning project; production setups would front this with CloudFront + Origin Access Control instead)

### Authentication: OIDC (not access keys)

Initially scoped with long-lived IAM user access keys, then deliberately switched to OpenID Connect (OIDC) federation - GitHub Actions assumes a short-lived AWS role per run instead of storing static secrets. This is the safer, more current best practice and avoids key rotation entirely.

- Registered token.actions.githubusercontent.com as an OIDC Identity Provider in IAM
- Created an IAM Role (github-actions-s3-deploy) with a trust policy scoped to this specific repo and branch
- Attached a least-privilege permissions policy: s3:PutObject, s3:DeleteObject, s3:ListBucket, scoped only to the project bucket
- Deleted the original IAM user (github-actions-deployer) and its access keys once OIDC was confirmed working

## 5.4 Trust Policy

{  
"Version": "2012-10-17",  
"Statement": \[  
{  
"Effect": "Allow",  
"Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",  
"Principal": {  
"Federated": "arn:aws:iam::&lt;ACCOUNT_ID&gt;:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com"  
},  
"Condition": {  
"StringEquals": {  
"token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud": \["sts.amazonaws.com"\]  
},  
"StringLike": {  
"token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": \[  
"repo:qezman/cicd-static-site:ref:refs/heads/main"  
\]  
}  
}  
}  
\]  
}

## ![](data:image/png;base64,)_IAM role trust relationship tab_

## 5.5 Final Workflow

name: Deploy Static Site to S3  
<br/>on:  
push:  
branches:  
\- main  
<br/>permissions:  
id-token: write  
contents: read  
<br/>jobs:  
deploy:  
runs-on: ubuntu-latest  
steps:  
\- name: Checkout repository  
uses: actions/checkout@v4  
<br/>\- name: Configure AWS credentials via OIDC  
uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4  
with:  
role-to-assume: \${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}  
aws-region: \${{ secrets.AWS_REGION }}  
<br/>\- name: Sync files to S3  
run: |  
aws s3 sync . s3://\${{ secrets.AWS_S3_BUCKET }} \\  
\--delete \\  
\--exclude ".git/\*" \\  
\--exclude ".github/\*"

## 5.6 GitHub Secrets Used (final)

- AWS_ROLE_ARN - the OIDC role ARN (arn:aws:iam::&lt;account&gt;:role/github-actions-s3-deploy)
- AWS_S3_BUCKET - target bucket name
- AWS_REGION - bucket's AWS region

**_Note:_** _AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY were used temporarily during initial setup and later deleted once OIDC was confirmed working - no long-lived credentials remain._

## 5.7 Troubleshooting Log

### Issue 1: Wrong action input name

Used aws-arn: as an input to aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials, which isn't a recognized input.

Fix: corrected to role-to-assume:, the actual input name the action expects.

### Issue 2: Node 20 deprecation warning

Informational only - GitHub Actions runners now default to Node 24; the warning does not fail the build and required no action.

### Issue 3: "Could not assume role with OIDC: Request ARN is invalid"

Cause: the AWS_ROLE_ARN secret contained a user ARN (arn:...:user/github-actions-deployer) instead of a role ARN. OIDC's AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity can only target IAM roles, which carry a trust policy - IAM users have no trust policy and cannot be assumed.

Fix: created the missing IAM Role (Web identity trust type, GitHub OIDC provider, scoped trust policy) and updated the secret with the correct role ARN (arn:...:role/github-actions-s3-deploy).

### Issue 4: Confusing trust policy with permissions policy

Pasted the trust policy JSON (which includes a Principal element) into the role's Permissions tab, which expects a policy with a Resource element instead. IAM error: "does not support the Principal element" / "Add a Resource element."

Fix: kept the trust policy under the Trust relationships tab, and added a separate least-privilege S3 permissions policy (with Resource, no Principal) under Permissions.

## 5.8 Cleanup

- Deleted AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY from GitHub Secrets
- Deleted the IAM user github-actions-deployer and its access keys from AWS
- Confirmed only AWS_ROLE_ARN, AWS_S3_BUCKET, AWS_REGION remain as secrets

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## 5.9 Screenshots

![](data:image/png;base64,)_Successful GitHub Actions run_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_S3 bucket contents after sync_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Live site at S3 endpoint_

## 5.10 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Fully automated deploy-on-push pipeline to S3, authenticated via short-lived OIDC-assumed role, no static credentials in use
- Blockers: Several config/syntax issues, all resolved (see 5.7)
- Key learning: IAM roles vs users behave very differently with OIDC - only roles carry trust policies and can be assumed. Also: trust policies and permissions policies are separate documents attached in separate places, and mixing them up produces IAM validation errors, not silent failures

# 6\. Day 11-14: Deploy to AWS S3 (verification & iteration)

## 6.1 Objective

Since the deployment mechanism was already built and proven in Day 7-10, this phase focused on verifying the pipeline's reliability under real edits rather than rebuilding it.

## 6.2 Tests Performed

### Test 1: End-to-end content update

Edited a visible line in index.html, committed, and pushed to main. Workflow triggered automatically and completed in 9 seconds. Change was reflected in the S3 bucket contents and confirmed live on the site - matched the local code exactly.

### Test 2: Deletion propagation

Deleted README.md locally, committed, and pushed. The --delete flag in the aws s3 sync command correctly removed the file from the S3 bucket as well, confirming the sync is a true mirror rather than additive-only.

## 6.3 Result

Full push-to-live loop confirmed working end-to-end: edits, additions, and deletions all sync correctly and quickly (~9s trigger-to-complete).

## 6.4 Screenshots

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_workflow run_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_S3 bucket after README.md deletion_

## 6.5 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Pipeline verified reliable for updates and deletions, fast turnaround (~9s)
- Blockers: None
- Key learning: Testing deletions, not just additions, is essential to confirm a sync-based deploy step behaves as a true mirror of the repo

# 7\. Day 15-17: Rollback/Error Strategy

## 7.1 Objective

Add a safety net so a bad push doesn't silently go live and stay live - combining prevention (stop bad deploys before they happen) with recovery (undo quickly if one gets through).

## 7.2 Layer 1: S3 Versioning (recovery)

Enabled Bucket Versioning under S3 → Properties. Every sync now creates new object versions instead of overwriting silently, so any previous version of any file can be restored directly from the S3 console without touching Git.

## 7.3 Layer 2: Git Revert (recovery, source of truth)

\$ git revert &lt;bad-commit-hash&gt;  
\$ git push origin main

Reverting triggers the pipeline again with the previous good state as the new commit - keeps Git history and the live site in sync, rather than only fixing S3 directly.

## 7.4 Layer 3: Pre-deploy Sanity Check (prevention)

Added a step to the workflow that runs after AWS credentials are configured but before the sync, so a failure stops the job before anything touches S3.

\- name: Sanity check before deploy  
run: |  
if \[ ! -s index.html \]; then  
echo "index.html missing or empty - aborting deploy"  
exit 1  
fi

## 7.5 Tests Performed

### Test 1: Git revert

Pushed a change, then ran git revert HEAD and pushed again. Confirmed the pipeline triggered a fresh deploy and the site returned to its prior state.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Test 2: Sanity check blocks a bad deploy

Emptied index.html intentionally, committed, and pushed. Workflow failed at the Sanity check step exactly as designed and never reached the sync step - S3 was untouched. Reverted the test commit afterward to restore the real site.

## 7.6 Result

Both the prevention check and the recovery paths (git revert, S3 versioning) were confirmed working, not just configured.

## 7.7 Screenshots

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_sanity check failing the workflow_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_S3 bucket versioning enabled_

## 7.8 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Three-layer safety net in place - S3 versioning, git revert, and a pre-deploy sanity check - all tested, not just configured
- Blockers: None
- Key learning: Prevention (sanity check) and recovery (versioning, revert) are complementary, not redundant - prevention reduces how often recovery is needed, but doesn't replace it

# 8\. Day 18-20: Custom Domain

## 8.1 Objective

Add a custom HTTPS domain to the site and use the opportunity to close a security gap from Day 7-10: the S3 bucket was originally public-read, which is acceptable for learning but not production-grade. This phase moves to a private bucket served only through CloudFront.

## 8.2 Architecture Decision

Chose to do this fully rather than skip it: registered domain (qossim005.online, via Namecheap) delegated to Route 53, ACM certificate for HTTPS, CloudFront distribution with Origin Access Control (OAC) in front of a now-private S3 bucket. This also sets up Day 21-22 log monitoring, since CloudFront provides proper access logs that a bare S3 static website endpoint does not.

## 8.3 Cross-Account DNS Complication

The domain's Route 53 hosted zone existed in a different AWS account (used for an earlier, separate project). Rather than moving infrastructure between accounts or duplicating the domain, used subdomain delegation:

- Created a new, separate hosted zone in this project's AWS account for cicd-demo.qossim005.online specifically
- Added a single one-time NS record in the other account's qossim005.online hosted zone, delegating authority for the cicd-demo subdomain to this account's new hosted zone
- From that point on, this account has full independent control over everything under cicd-demo.qossim005.online - no further cross-account steps needed

**_Note:_** _This is a clean pattern worth reusing: subdomain delegation lets one AWS account own DNS for a piece of a domain without needing access to the parent zone's account at all beyond the single delegating NS record._

## 8.4 ACM Certificate

- Requested in us-east-1 (N. Virginia) - required for CloudFront regardless of the bucket's actual region
- Domain: cicd-demo.qossim005.online
- DNS validation, using the "Create records in Route 53" one-click option - worked directly since the hosted zone was now in the same account as the certificate

## 8.5 CloudFront Distribution

- Origin: S3 bucket (REST API endpoint, not the website endpoint - required for Origin Access Control)
- Origin access: Origin Access Control (OAC), newly created
- Security: no WAF - unnecessary cost/complexity for a small static project
- Alternate domain name (CNAME): cicd-demo.qossim005.online, attached after cert issued
- Default root object: index.html

## 8.6 Locking Down the Bucket

Generated a scoped bucket policy allowing only CloudFront's service principal, conditioned on this specific distribution's ARN:

{  
"Version": "2012-10-17",  
"Statement": \[  
{  
"Sid": "AllowCloudFrontServicePrincipal",  
"Effect": "Allow",  
"Principal": { "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com" },  
"Action": "s3:GetObject",  
"Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::cicd-static-site-560205084952/\*",  
"Condition": {  
"StringEquals": {  
"AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::560205084952:distribution/EPTIKAWY5CP1E"  
}  
}  
}  
\]  
}

- Replaced the old public-read policy from Day 7 entirely
- Re-enabled "Block all public access" on the bucket - safe now since CloudFront authenticates via OAC, not as an anonymous public request

## 8.7 Troubleshooting Log

### Issue 1: Cross-account confusion

Initial uncertainty about whether ACM and Route 53 needed to be in the same account. Clarified: AWS accounts are fully isolated - a certificate in one account cannot auto-validate against a hosted zone in another. Resolved via the subdomain delegation approach in 8.3, avoiding any ongoing cross-account dependency.

### Issue 2: Console wizard mismatch

CloudFront's newer console flow (Get started → Specify origin → Enable security → Get TLS certificate → Review and create) differs from older documentation/guides that describe a single-page form. The "Get TLS certificate" step was skipped by the wizard in practice; resolved by creating the distribution first with the default \*.cloudfront.net domain, then attaching the custom domain and certificate afterward via Distribution → General → Edit.

### Issue 3: Route 53 A record for the CloudFront alias

The ACM validation CNAME alone does not point traffic at the site - a separate Alias A record (blank name = zone apex, i.e. cicd-demo.qossim005.online itself, routing to the CloudFront distribution) was required in the same hosted zone. Easy to miss since it's a distinct record from the validation CNAME.

## 8.8 Verification

- Old S3 website endpoint confirmed no longer publicly reachable (403) - proves the lockdown worked
- CloudFront distribution confirmed Deployed, with custom SSL certificate attached (not the default CloudFront cert)
- <https://cicd-demo.qossim005.online> loads the site correctly over HTTPS with a valid certificate

## 8.9 Screenshots

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_CloudFront distribution settings_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_Route 53 hosted zone records for cicd-demo_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_live HTTPS site_

## 8.10 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Site served over HTTPS on a custom subdomain, S3 bucket fully private, only reachable via CloudFront + OAC
- Blockers: Cross-account DNS, CloudFront console wizard differences, missing alias A record - all resolved
- Key learning: Subdomain delegation is a clean way to give one AWS account DNS authority over part of a domain without ongoing cross-account dependency. Also: CloudFront + OAC + a private bucket is meaningfully more secure than a public S3 website endpoint, and the migration between the two is straightforward once the pieces (cert, distribution, bucket policy) are understood individually

# 9\. Day 21-22: Log Monitoring

## 9.1 Objective

Add visibility into site traffic and errors, taking advantage of CloudFront now being in front of the site (a bare S3 website endpoint has no built-in logging). Two complementary layers were set up: raw access logs for detailed after-the-fact review, and real-time metrics/alerting for immediate awareness of problems.

## 9.2 Standard Logging (CloudFront → S3)

Note: this required navigating to the distribution's Logging tab, under Access log destinations - not General → Edit as in older documentation, reflecting a recent CloudFront console update.

- Created access log delivery under Distribution → Logging → Access log destinations
- Destination: Amazon S3, using a new, separate bucket dedicated to logs (kept apart from the site's own bucket so logs are never served as content and never touched by the deploy pipeline's --delete sync)
- Left field selection and output format at defaults

**_Note:_** _A separate 'Attached real-time access logs' feature also exists on this tab (Kinesis-based, near-instant delivery) but was intentionally left disabled - it requires its own Kinesis stream and additional cost, and isn't necessary for a project at this scale._

## 9.3 CloudWatch Metrics (automatic, no setup required)

Confirmed CloudFront automatically reports six metrics per distribution to CloudWatch with zero configuration: Requests, 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate, TotalErrorRate, BytesDownloaded, BytesUploaded.

## 9.4 CloudWatch Alarm

- Metric: 5xxErrorRate for this distribution
- Statistic: Average, Period: 5 minutes
- Condition: greater than 5%
- Notification: new SNS topic created, subscribed with email, confirmed via the confirmation email link
- Alarm name: cicd-demo-high-5xx-error-rate

This ties directly into the Day 15-17 rollback strategy - a bad deploy that causes server errors now triggers an email alert rather than going unnoticed.

## 9.5 Screenshots

## ![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

_access log destination configured_

![](data:image/png;base64,)

_CloudWatch alarm_

## 9.6 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Two-layer monitoring in place - raw access logs in S3 for detailed review, CloudWatch alarm + email alert for real-time error awareness
- Blockers: CloudFront console had moved logging to a dedicated Logging tab rather than General → Edit, and a similarly named but unrelated 'real-time access logs' (Kinesis-based) feature initially caused confusion - resolved by distinguishing the two
- Key learning: AWS console layouts and feature sets shift over time (e.g. CloudFront's newer Free/Pro/pay-as-you-go plans affect logging availability) - worth verifying current documentation rather than assuming a guide matches the live console exactly

# 10\. Day 23: Final Polish

## 10.1 Objective

Close out remaining loose ends before final review: a proper repo-level README, one last end-to-end smoke test, and a quick review of the repo for leftover artifacts from troubleshooting sessions.

## 10.2 Repo README

Added a README.md to the repository root covering: project overview, architecture diagram (text form), tech stack, deployment flow, security design, rollback strategy, monitoring setup, and a replication guide for anyone rebuilding this from scratch. Kept separate from this document - the README is a concise repo-facing summary, this document is the full detailed walkthrough.

## 10.3 Final Smoke Test

- Pushed a final small change to main and confirmed it flowed through the pipeline automatically to the live HTTPS URL
- Confirmed the CloudWatch 5xxErrorRate alarm remained healthy (no errors) throughout
- Reviewed the repository for leftover test commits/files from earlier troubleshooting (e.g. the intentionally-broken commit from Day 15-17) - left in place as they're part of the genuine history and don't affect the working state

## 10.4 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Repository is complete, documented, and passes a final end-to-end smoke test
- Blockers: None

# 11\. Day 24: Documentation

## 11.1 Objective

Ensure the project is properly documented for both the internship deliverable and anyone else who might pick this project up.

## 11.2 Deliverables

- CICD_Devops_Journey.docx (this document) - full day-by-day technical walkthrough, including every command, every configuration decision, and every issue encountered with its resolution
- README.md - concise, repo-facing summary: architecture, tech stack, security design, and a replication guide
- Progress tracker (Section 2) - at-a-glance status of all 25 days

## 11.3 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done
- Outcome: Documentation completed alongside the build, checkpoint by checkpoint, rather than reconstructed after the fact - this kept detail accurate and current at each stage
- Blockers: None

# 12\. Day 25: Final Review

## 12.1 Full Pipeline Review

- Version control: Git fundamentals, branch → PR → merge workflow
- Static site: simple HTML/CSS, deployed as-is
- CI/CD: GitHub Actions workflow, triggered on push to main
- Cloud hosting: AWS S3, chosen over Netlify to build transferable AWS skills
- Authentication: OIDC federation, no long-lived credentials at any point in the final state
- Reliability: S3 versioning, git revert path, pre-deploy sanity check - all tested, not just configured
- Custom domain & HTTPS: ACM + CloudFront + Origin Access Control, private S3 bucket
- Monitoring: CloudFront access logs + CloudWatch alarm with email alerting
- Documentation: full technical walkthrough + repo README

## 12.2 What Went Well

- The core pipeline (Day 7-14) worked cleanly once the OIDC role/user distinction was sorted out
- Choosing S3 over Netlify paid off directly in Day 18-22, where CloudFront's logging and custom domain capabilities built naturally on the S3 setup
- Testing failure paths deliberately (sanity check blocking a bad deploy, deletion syncing correctly) caught what configuration alone wouldn't have proven

## 12.3 Challenges & Resolutions

- IAM role vs IAM user confusion with OIDC - resolved by understanding that only roles carry trust policies and can be assumed
- Cross-account DNS for the custom domain - resolved cleanly via subdomain delegation, avoiding any ongoing cross-account dependency
- AWS console UI changes (CloudFront's newer wizard flow, relocated logging controls) not matching some initial guidance - resolved by checking the live console directly rather than relying on assumptions

## 12.4 Possible Next Steps (beyond this project's scope)

- Convert the manually-built infrastructure to Terraform, now that the manual build has built the underlying mental model
- Move from a public S3 bucket policy to full private-by-default from day one in a future project, rather than upgrading partway through
- Explore CloudFront Functions for lightweight edge logic (e.g. redirects, header rewrites)

## 12.5 Final Outcome

An automated pipeline that builds and deploys a website, giving hands-on insight into version control, CI/CD concepts, and real AWS DevOps practices. What began as a beginner-level exercise using GitHub Actions and S3 grew into a fuller demonstration of production-adjacent practices: OIDC-based CI/CD authentication, CDN + HTTPS via CloudFront and ACM, a private origin bucket, tested rollback strategy, and proper monitoring with alerting.

## 12.6 Checkpoint Summary

- Status: Done - project complete
- All 25 days documented and verified

# 13\. Post-Launch Incident: DNS Outage

## 13.1 Summary

After the project was marked complete, the live site (<https://cicd-demo.qossim005.online>) went down unexpectedly following an unrelated infrastructure change on a separate project sharing the same parent domain (qossim005.online).

## 13.2 Root Cause

The parent domain's nameservers were intentionally updated at the registrar (Namecheap) to point to a new, Terraform-managed Route 53 hosted zone, as part of work on a separate project. This is a legitimate and expected change for that project - however, it silently broke DNS resolution for this project's subdomain, because the NS delegation record for cicd-demo (originally created in the old hosted zone during Day 18-20) only existed in the old, now-bypassed zone. The new zone had no record telling it to hand off authority for cicd-demo to this project's hosted zone, so requests for cicd-demo.qossim005.online resolved to nothing.

**_Note:_** _Key mechanism: delegation is a one-way pointer stored as an NS record in the parent zone. Changing which zone is authoritative for the parent domain does not automatically carry over delegations that were only recorded in the old zone - they must be recreated in whatever zone is newly authoritative._

## 13.3 Diagnosis

- Confirmed via dig NS qossim005.online that the parent domain's authoritative nameservers had changed
- Compared against terraform output nameservers from the other project, confirming the new zone was the intended, correct one for that project
- Checked the new zone's existing records and confirmed no NS delegation record for cicd-demo was present - this was the missing piece, not a misconfiguration elsewhere

## 13.4 Resolution

- Retrieved the 4 NS values from this project's own cicd-demo.qossim005.online hosted zone (unchanged since Day 18 - a hosted zone's own NS record does not change when its parent's nameservers change)
- Created a new NS record in the new, now-authoritative parent zone: name cicd-demo, type NS, value = those same 4 nameservers
- Verified resolution with dig NS cicd-demo.qossim005.online, then confirmed the live site was reachable again

**_Note:_** _The old hosted zone's NS record for cicd-demo was left in place, orphaned but harmless, since nothing queries that zone anymore. It can be deleted for hygiene, but was not required for the fix._

## 13.5 Key Learning

Subdomain delegation via NS records is resilient to changes within either zone individually, but not to a change in which zone is authoritative for the parent domain - that specific change requires re-adding the delegation record in the new parent zone. This is an easy failure mode when a domain is shared across multiple unrelated projects/accounts, and is worth checking any time a parent domain's nameservers are modified for any reason.

This also reinforced the value of the monitoring set up in Day 21-22: while DNS-level outages aren't caught by the CloudFront 5xxErrorRate alarm (the request never reaches CloudFront at all), the incident was caught quickly through direct manual verification of the live URL - a good argument for adding an external uptime check (e.g. a simple periodic HTTP check from outside AWS) as a future improvement.

## 13.6 Incident Summary

- Detected: site found down during unrelated work on a separate project
- Cause: NS delegation record for a subdomain not carried over when the parent domain's authoritative hosted zone changed
- Resolution time: same session, resolved once the missing delegation record was identified and recreated
- Follow-up action identified: consider adding an external uptime/DNS monitoring check independent of AWS-internal metrics
